So, let's look closely at how this works with some other brands, alright? And you'll see that the brand script shows up in almost all effective marketing. This is all state insurance and all state insurance, we've got an ad, it's a print ad, and it's a woman, she's very happy because she just saved a bunch of money on insurance. Okay, so this one picture actually takes up two spaces on the grid. One she's a character looking for insurance, so we got that covered. Second she's happy, so we've covered success. One print ad covers two spaces on the grid. And by the way, every ad does not have to include all seven categories. But nothing you communicate should come from anything else than these seven categories. Nothing, nothing. If you do it's noise. So she's happy, we've covered two of them. Next we've got a problem. You've got a villain, and that villain is mayhem. And that villain is going to externally, internally and philosophically take her life down unless she gets some insurance. Then we've got a guide, and the guide is competent, has incredible authority, comes off as a caring person, he's the former president from the show 24, how can we not trust him? And then we've got a plan, it's the all state value plan, and if we take their plan, it will be our path to experiencing a better life. The call to action on their website is get a quote, it is not passive, it is not learn more or get started. It's strong and it's direct. Now I think they're a little weak on the success bucket. They have a viral video about how life can be great if you're not worried about consequences, you know if you have insurance, they need to stronger play on that. But we know they've got really strong on failure. By the way, they can get away with a lot more on failure because they use humor. If they didn't use humor and make it funny, they would not be able to get away with the tragic consequences that they're talking about. So humor lightens the load psychologically. Here's another brand, this brand is CarMax. CarMax is where you go to buy a used car, but CarMax was smart enough to know that people buying a used car are more motivated to resolve an internal problem than an external problem. We want a car, but we don't want to have to deal with this guy. We don't want to have to deal with a used car dealer. So they have removed the car dealer from the equation and they have positioned themselves as the guide. The guide does not have to be a physical person, it can just be the brand as it is with CarMax. Their plan is a three-step plan. In fact, if you go into a CarMax showroom, there's three-step plans for everything. For buying the car, for selling the car, for getting insurance, there's three-step plans for everything. The call to action is clear on their website. In fact, their whole website has been story branded. If you want to see a great example of story brand websites in motion, go to CarMax.com. Then success is I get a car, I feel good about it, I didn't buy a lemon. The failure is I have to deal with a used car dealer and I bought a lemon. How's this working for them? $8.75 billion a year in used cars. They're larger than the next three competitors combined. They're extremely clear about the problem that they solve. What questions, keep looking at your screen. What questions do we need to answer in order to create clear messaging? Our brand script is going to guide us through these seven areas of questions. What does your customers want? What do they want? What problem is your customer experiencing? External, internal, philosophical? Have you positioned your brand as the guide to the hero? Have you created a clear plan for the hero to win the day? Are your calls to action clear? Have you explained what your customer's life will look like if they do business with you? Have you explained the negative consequences of not doing business with you? If you want, take a screenshot of that or pull out your phone and take a picture of it because those are the questions that you want to answer in this brand script. This is going to take you a little bit of time. I strongly recommend that you pick up building a story brand, my book Building a Story Brand, get the audio version and just listen to it over and over. The whole book just helps you fill out your brand script. That's all the book does. I don't get money from that book. That money goes to a scholarship at Lipscomb University to help underserved kids. I don't mind pitching my book even if I do get the money, but that money goes to a good cause. Grab that book and it's really going to help you if you want more time thinking through this. What we want eventually is a brand script for our overall brand. It looks like this and you'll fill that out and then you actually want a brand script for every division of your company and then you want a brand script for every product and every division of your company. When you achieve this, you will achieve clear communication. You also have to be exceptionally disciplined about staying on message, exceptionally disciplined about staying on message, repeating the same words in the same way over and over until your audience has it memorized. I don't know. Give me jazz hands if you've ever branded a cow. I'm in Texas today, so probably a lot of you in Texas have branded a cow. Jazz hands if you've ever branded a cow. Nobody's branded a cow. I have a buddy who has a ranch out in Oregon. He invited me out to his ranch one day to brand cattle. It was a really crazy fun experience. You lay over the back of that calf and you take, it's actually an electric heated up brand and you brand the back of that calf. Thankfully, I'm an animal lover so I wouldn't love on that idea, but then they don't actually feel much of anything. They kind of just feel like they got bit by a fly or something. What would happen if I took a different cattle brand and I put it over the top of my buddy's brand and then a different cattle brand, I put it over the top of that brand? Pretty soon you've got four or five different brands all over the top of each other. You would just have a mess and nobody would know whose cow that belongs to. If you aren't using the exact same messaging in the exact same words, repeating them over and over, taking your customers through an exercise and memorization, that's how you are branding. You are branding in a very confusing way and don't expect anybody to figure out what you do. Don't expect anybody to figure out what you do. We have got to clarify our message. Filling out that brand script is paramount. The question we've got to ask here before lunch is this. Is the story we are telling clear and compelling? If yours is not, you just went through two hours in which I introduced a process that will help you make your story very clear and very compelling. It is super, super important. We've worked with a brand called Chick-fil-A. They make chicken sandwiches in restaurants all over the country. I married Betsy seven, eight years ago and she is the oldest of seven. When her parents were in their late 50s, they adopted an infant because they were fostering this child, fell in love with the child, adopted an infant. Now Gloria is seven years old and they pretty much don't take her to fast food very much but they go to Chick-fil-A. When we were working with Chick-fil-A, I said, Lori, why Chick-fil-A? She said, well, it is the only place I can go to without feeling guilty about myself. I thought that is interesting. She is going there to resolve an internal problem, not an external problem. That is fascinating. If you think about what Chick-fil-A has done externally, what is the problem that Chick-fil-A solves? Hunger. They solve lunch. That is what they resolve. And dinner. And apparently breakfast. That is what they resolve. Internally, what problem do they resolve? Well, how do you feel about yourself when you walk into a quick serve restaurant or a fast food restaurant? I don't feel very good about myself at all. Every once in a while I am on the road, in my rental car, late to a meeting and I have to grab lunch quick. I will walk into some fast food restaurant. And I always think the same thing to myself as I walk in. I think it has come to this. This day has spiraled so far out of control, we are going to do this to our body. And then you are standing there at the counter and you are talking to somebody. They don't want to be here. You don't want to be here. None of us want to be here. I feel like I am supporting some kind of human trafficking just trying to get a cheeseburg. It is awful. And what Chick-fil-A has done is they have resolved the internal internal philosophical problem. Externally, you are going to get lunch. You are going to get solve hunger. Internally, you don't have to feel bad about yourself for eating here because we have got some healthy options. And also the people behind the counter, which is the real thing, like being there. They want to be there. They are treated well. They have given great opportunities. They say things like my pleasure. Philosophically, they are closed on Sundays. The opportunity cost on being closed one day on a weekend is insanely high. Insanely high. No other fast food place would do that. What are they saying to the community? They are saying we care about more than just money. They happen to be religious so they are saying some other religious statements as well. But even if you are not religious, they are saying we care about our people more than we care about making money. We are going to close one day a week and give them some rest. What is happening to Lori, my mother-in-law, at the counter? And she gives that $5 for the chicken sandwich. She is resolving an external problem of hunger and internal problem of I do not have to feel bad about myself or bring my daughter here. And philosophically, I am associating my values with the values of this organization. She never realizes it is happening. But it creates rabid fans. The average Burger King, 1.5 million per store per year open on Sunday. The average McDonald's, 2.5 million per store per year open on Sunday. The average Chick-fil-A closed on Sunday, 5.2 million per store per year. I was just with those guys a month or two ago. Their number one problem right now, the number one problem at Chick-fil-A is their coolers are too small in their restaurants. They physically can't hold the products that they sell in a day. And they are running out of food at 3 and 4 o'clock and having to shut down their restaurants. How are they doing it? They are inviting customers into a story in which they experience a climactic scene and their lives are better for having done it. And they are being richly rewarded at the cash register for doing so. The work that you are going to have to do to create a compelling story is going to take some time. But I want you to fill out that brand script. And then once you fill it out, I want you to make it better and better and better and better until you start getting the responses that you are looking for. I started my company basically six years ago. We went to $5 million before we ever put a single dollar into advertising. And we grew quickly because we had a message that spread. If you have a clear message, it will spread infinitely further than a muddled message. You know, I got a call from a lawyer in Nashville, Tennessee two years ago. He said, Don, I was giving your name. You worked with a friend of mine. I'm going to run for Senate in the state of Tennessee as a Democrat. I'm going to be in the Democratic primary. I said, OK, interesting because it's a Republican state. And my friend said, you could help me. And I said, well, maybe. I don't help a lot of folks with that. But tell me about yourself. What are you running for? Why are you running? Because, Don, I'm running because the country needs to be more kind. We're just not a kind place anymore. I believe in kindness. I'm also an army vet. And I flew a Black Hawk helicopter in Afghanistan. I got all these awards. Went on and on about those awards. I said, look, thank you very much for your service. When I've helped people who've been military heroes like you have, one of the temptations that they have is they keep talking about their military service and themselves. And they forget that the best thing to do is actually to talk about the customer and their needs. And he said, well, you know my story. He was insecure about running for Senate. And he needed to justify why you should elect him. It's because he flew a Black Hawk helicopter. We love our heroes in this country. We honor them. We just don't vote for them. Bob Dole was a war hero. He lost. John McCain was a war hero. He lost. John Kerry was a war hero. He lost. All the people with limited to no military experience. George Bush went missing for a year. And he won. Why did he win? Because he didn't talk about his military service. He talked about the people. We vote for guides, not heroes. His name is James. And I said, James, I wish I were with you. I don't have time. I'm trying to build a company. But man, if you ever have any last minute questions, please. But remember, don't talk about yourself so much. Talk about the customer. Well, I lost touch with James. Never heard anything about the campaign. And tell about, what, two, three weeks ago? Not long ago. Maybe, I don't know, maybe two months ago now, though. James spent $1.9 million on his Democratic primary for Senate. And he was beaten by a woman in Memphis who spent $12,000. $12,000 in advertising beat $1.9 million in advertising. Her name was, I just voted for her, actually. I shouldn't have said that out loud. I just voted for her. Her name is Markita Robinson, I think. And my wife and I were laying in bed going, what in God's name happened? So I Google this woman's name. She comes up on the screen, on YouTube or whatever, with her acceptance speech for accepting the Democratic nomination for Senate. And she says, she's got a mask on. And she's saying, thank you so much for this. It's shocking to win with this little effort. We are going to need more money. But this election is not about me. This election is about the families of Tennessee and what their struggles are and the pain that they're dealing with. I don't want anybody talking about me or the fact that I won this primary. We have got to talk about the struggling families in Tennessee. We're going to talk about their pain. And we're going to take their pain to Washington, DC. We're going to do something about it. I think we actually sent her money, because she's the guide, not the hero. Play the guide in the story, not the hero. Your customers are struggling. They're in pain. And you have a solution to their pain and their problem. Begin positioning your product as the solution to their problem. And you're going to start doing more business.
